title: Fixing Broken HTML Table Structures with Invalid Headings

statement.markdown: Given a string representing an HTML table structure, validate if the table has a valid heading structure. A valid heading structure is defined as a table with a single `<thead>` and a single `<tbody>`, each containing at least one `<tr>` element, and all `<th>` elements must come before any `<td>` elements in a row. If the table structure is invalid, return an error message indicating the issue.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing the HTML table structure
statement.outputFormat: A string indicating whether the table structure is valid or an error message
statement.notes: Assume the input string only contains valid HTML tags and attributes.

constraints.description: The maximum number of rows in the table

examples.explanation: A valid table structure with headings and rows

examples.output: Error: Invalid heading structure - <th> elements must come before <td> elements
examples.explanation: An invalid table structure with headings and rows in the wrong order

hints.text: Parse the HTML string to extract the table structure
Check for the presence of a single <thead> and <tbody> element
Verify that all <th> elements come before <td> elements in each row

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Broken HTML Table Structures with Invalid Headings
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to correctly parse the HTML string and extract the table structure.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach is to use a regular expression to match the table structure and check for the presence of a single <thead> and <tbody> element.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to use an HTML parser to extract the table structure and then check for the validity of the headings and rows.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a library like Cheerio to parse the HTML string and then use a recursive function to traverse the table structure and check for the validity of the headings and rows.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The key to proving the correctness of this solution is to show that it correctly identifies and reports invalid table structures.
commonMistakes: Failing to check for the presence of <th> elements before <td> elements
